entities.description: The immediate source edition for CME00088. Garrett prints a short comparative preface and a direct transcription of two distinct verse works from Add MS 17866, numbering their continuous sequence as one.

rights.basis: Published in 1911, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.

dateStatements.label: Garrett's palaeographic objection
dateStatements.notes: The normalized interval expresses the arithmetic implication of Garrett's statement and is not a modern repository date.
dateStatements.value: at least about 150 years later than 1272-1327

entities.description: Garrett's direct source manuscript. The two verse works run continuously on ff. 5r-21v and share f. 16r as the ending surface of the herbal and opening surface of the receipt collection.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 17866
notes: The current catalog supplies no linked digitized content. This is a dated access finding, not proof that no preservation photography exists.
Garrett's a/b notation is retained as historical recto/verso notation: ff. 5a-21b correspond to ff. 5r-21v.

physicalDescription.extent: The catalog describes one item and contents extending at least through f. 42; the Garrett sequence occupies ff. 5r-21v.
physicalDescription.format: Octavo; Garrett describes careful writing and rubrication
notes: The British Library record broadly describes medicinal virtues, prescriptions, charms, an earlier verse portion beginning at f. 3, and a Latin-English Nomina herbarum at f. 42.
DIMEV and eLALME associate this copy's scribal language with Lincolnshire. That witness-level localization is not a composition place for either abstract work.

rights.basis: The texts are attested in medieval manuscripts and far predate U.S. copyright terms.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous medieval herbal and medical-receipt texts
notes: This does not determine rights or contractual terms in modern editions, encodings, catalog prose, manuscript photographs, or interface assets.

bibliographic.citation: Ferdinand Holthausen, 'Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift,' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 18 (Neue Folge 6) (1896): 293-331.
bibliographic.title: Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift

entities.description: Holthausen's newly collated edition of the two verse works in KB X 90. The receipt poem is printed first as 'Gereimte Heilkunde' and the herbal second as 'Heilkräuterlehre,' the reverse of Add MS 17866.
entities.label: Ferdinand Holthausen, Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift (1896)

rights.basis: Published in 1896, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.
rights.component: 1896 edition, transcription, emendations, and editorial matter

rights.basis: Holthausen died in 1956. Swedish Copyright Act section 43 gives the ordinary term through the end of the seventieth year after the author's death, yielding a cautious end date of 2026-12-31 for protectable contributions.
rights.component: Any transcription choices, emendations, notes, or other editorial contribution satisfying the applicable originality threshold
notes: This is a term calculation, not a conclusion that every diplomatic transcription detail is sufficiently original to be protected; re-review on or after 2027-01-01.

bibliographic.citation: George Stephens, 'Extracts in Prose and Verse from an Old English Medical Manuscript, Preserved in the Royal Library at Stockholm,' Archaeologia 30 (1844): 349-418; separately issued, London: J. B. Nichols and Son, 1844.
bibliographic.title: Extracts in Prose and Verse from an Old English Medical Manuscript, Preserved in the Royal Library at Stockholm

entities.description: The first verified print edition of material from KB X 90 and the edition against which Holthausen performed his new manuscript collation. Stephens prints the receipt collection at Archaeologia pp. 349-363, the herbal at pp. 364-393, and a duplicated continuation of receipt material at pp. 393-395 before turning to prose extracts.
entities.label: George Stephens, Extracts in Prose and Verse from an Old English Medical Manuscript (1844)

rights.basis: Published in 1844, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.
rights.component: 1844 edition, transcription, and editorial matter

access.accessMethod: View or download the complete separate offprint.
access.entityLabel: Internet Archive scan of Stephens's 1844 separate offprint

localCopies.label: Complete Internet Archive PDF of Stephens 1844
notes: Exact hard-link reuse of the verified CME00099 Internet Archive PDF; source URL, byte count, SHA-256, and inode identity were checked.

workPortion.label: Stephens's editions of the two X 90 poems compared by Holthausen and Garrett
locators: receipt collection: Archaeologia pp. 349-363 / offprint pp. 1-15 / PDF pp. 7-21
herbal: Archaeologia pp. 364-393 / offprint pp. 16-45 / PDF pp. 22-51
duplicated receipt continuation: Archaeologia pp. 393-395 / offprint pp. 45-47 / PDF pp. 51-53
notes: The herbal ends with Stephens line 1425 on Archaeologia p. 393; a receipt passage then repeats and continues without a new division through p. 395.

Holthausen 1896, p. 294, explicitly identifies this duplication and reorganizes it into his first poem. DIMEV v1.0's StockhX90 entry for record 4171 cites Stephens pp. 394-395, a locator that does not match the directly inspected herbal opening and run on pp. 364-393.

access.accessMethod: Read the item metadata embedded in the official IIIF manifest or use the Yale Digital Collections page.
access.format: Digital Collections item record, MODS, and IIIF metadata

entities.description: A complete collateral witness preserving the same two Middle English verse works continuously at ff. 3r-20v, followed by Middle English and Latin prose herbal material. Direct image inspection identifies the herbal on ff. 3r-14v and the receipt collection beginning on the shared f. 14v and ending on f. 20v.

holding.notes: The IIIF metadata's Call Number value is 'Takamiya MS 46 1,' while its preferred citation and current collection usage identify Takamiya MS 46; both forms are preserved.

entities.label: Beinecke Library Takamiya MS 46

physicalDescription.extent: 43 leaves; the paired verse works occupy ff. 3r-20v
physicalDescription.format: One hand; single columns of 33 lines; English bookhand
notes: The official record dates and locates production in England and describes Middle English and Latin herbal material, red initials, headings, and words, and a modern vellum-board binding.

Yale describes the two opening poems as showing traces of East Anglian dialect; DIMEV/eLALME associates the historical Takamiya copy with southern Lincolnshire. These are witness-level scribal-language claims, not work composition places.

rights.basis: The manuscript is dated ca. 1400-1425 and the underlying texts are far outside U.S. copyright terms.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous medieval herbal and medical-receipt texts

access.accessMethod: Browse or download the complete Yale manuscript sequence using the item page, IIIF manifest, or generated PDF.
access.entityLabel: Yale Digital Collections complete facsimile of Takamiya MS 46
access.format: Full-color IIIF page images, auditable ZIP bundle, and generated PDF

localCopies.label: Complete 114-canvas Yale facsimile of Takamiya MS 46
notes: Complete for all provider-exposed surfaces: covers, pastedowns, front and rear endleaves, ff. 1r-43v, and the laid-in parchment fragment recto and verso. Yale exposes no separately labeled spine or edge views.
The exact IIIF Presentation 3 manifest, 114 full-size images, and per-image URLs, sizes, and SHA-256 values are retained in the bundle inventory.
Representative images are full-color sRGB JPEGs around 2,200 x 3,760 pixels and visibly preserve parchment tone, red rubrics, initials, corrections, and binding features.

workPortion.label: The two CME00088 works within complete Takamiya MS 46
notes: The two works share f. 14v / canvas 34. The herbal closes with 'This medicine is proved well a plight'; the red rubric 'For the hede warke' and incipit 'If a man or woman more or lesse' begin the receipt collection on the same surface.
The opening red rubric 'Hic incipiunt virtutes herbarum' and first herbal lines appear on f. 3r / canvas 11; the receipt collection ends on f. 20v / canvas 46.

rights.basis: Yale's public-domain policy says Yale claims no rights in public-domain works and Beinecke welcomes public-domain use, while the exact IIIF item gives a general user-liability warning rather than an item license.

localCopies.label: Complete DIMEV version 1.0 source release
notes: Exact hard-link reuse of the verified DIMEV v1.0 release archive; the Zenodo source URL, byte count, SHA-256, release commit, inode identity, and ZIP integrity were checked.

access.provider: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

rights.basis: The versioned release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.
rights.component: DIMEV version 1.0 data release

entities.description: The version 1.0 Digital Index of Middle English Verse records that separate the herbal and receipt collection, enumerate their wider witnesses and editions, and document the continuous Add MS 17866 boundary.
entities.label: DIMEV records 4171 and 2343
notes: Record 4171 lists eighteen active manuscript witnesses plus historical ghost records; record 2343 lists six witnesses.

Record 2343 represents Takamiya ff. 3r-20v as one sequence beginning with the herbal and ending with the receipts. Direct inspection of the red rubric on f. 14v establishes the component boundary without forcing the census record's broader witness unit onto the two-work cataloging model.
DIMEV's wider witnesses belong to the transmission histories and are not retroactively made sources of Garrett's Add MS 17866 transcription.

rights.basis: The versioned release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.
rights.component: DIMEV version 1.0 data release

next_steps: Ask the British Library to reconcile its fourteenth-century record with Garrett's much later estimate and the MED's ?c1450 citation form.

Obtain a complete color whole-object reproduction with covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, and edges where offered, then verify the ff. 5r-21v and shared f. 16r mapping directly.
Preserve any repository foliation concordance and item-specific reuse terms with the delivery.
openQuestions.question: What production date, full collation, hands, exact total extent, and modern foliation does current specialist cataloging assign to Add MS 17866?

next_steps: Retain KB's written response to the submitted enquiry and any quotation or delivery inventory.
Ask whether MfH 428 or newer preservation photography can supply every physical surface, and map the two poem portions separately.
Do not commit supplied images until the item-specific license and contractual terms are recorded.
openQuestions.question: Will KB supply a complete color facsimile of X 90, and what exact storage, Git redistribution, publication, and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand terms will apply?

next_steps: Ask Beinecke for written clarification keyed to object 16156709 and the project's public Git and print-on-demand purposes.
Meanwhile keep the complete bundle Git-ignored, cite and link the public object, and retain Yale's exact item rights warning and preferred citation.
Distinguish use of the public-domain medieval text and observations derived from the facsimile from redistribution of Yale's image files.
openQuestions.question: Does Yale consider Git redistribution of the complete Takamiya MS 46 facsimile and downstream commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand use to fall within its open-access policy for public-domain works?

next_steps: Consult current critical work on DIMEV 4171 and 2343 and collate the three complete paired witnesses.
Keep the visually verified Takamiya f. 14v shared boundary while investigating why DIMEV 2343 records the whole Takamiya ff. 3r-20v sequence and record 4171 does not list it separately.
Do not infer direct copying between the surviving manuscripts from shared order or readings alone.
openQuestions.question: What exact textual relationships connect the two works across Add MS 17866, X 90, Takamiya MS 46, and the wider DIMEV witness censuses?

next_steps: Report the apparent locator conflict to DIMEV with the record, edition key, and directly inspected page mapping.
Retain Stephens's raw printed sequence and Holthausen's explicit duplication analysis rather than silently normalizing either source.
Do not treat the conflicting citation as evidence that the herbal begins on p. 394.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV v1.0 cite Stephens 1844, pp. 394-395, for the X 90 witness of record 4171 when direct inspection places the herbal on Archaeologia pp. 364-393 and the duplicated receipt continuation on pp. 393-395?

next_steps: Review LALME/eLALME profiles 1.100 and 1.161 and current linguistic scholarship for authorial rather than scribal localization.
Preserve the British Library, Garrett, MED, Yale, and KB dates as separately attributed witness claims.
Do not turn Add MS 17866's Lincolnshire profile, Yale's East Anglian description, or a witness holding into a place of composition.
openQuestions.question: Where and when were the two works composed, as distinct from the scribal languages and production dates of their witnesses?

next_steps: Inspect CME repository history and ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for item-level production records beyond the collection-wide manual-keying description.
openQuestions.question: What exact U-M release, keying record, and transformation history produced the pinned CME00088 XML blob?

relations.subjectLabel: Ferdinand Holthausen, Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift (1896)

assertion.notes: Holthausen praises Stephens's edition as generally careful but reports collating it against the codex and correcting its text.

object.description: Stephens's earlier printing of the same X 90 poems

relations.subjectLabel: Ferdinand Holthausen, Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift (1896)

assertion.notes: Holthausen identifies Stephens lines 1426-1449 as a duplicate of receipt lines 7-30 and moves that passage plus the following 35 receipt lines into his reorganized Gereimte Heilkunde. The overlapping printed page ranges therefore describe textual duplication in X 90, not a third abstract work.

object.description: The X 90 receipt collection and herbal, including the manuscript's duplicated receipt passage
locators: duplicated receipt passage begins after the herbal on manuscript p. 78

subject.description: The two verse works in their raw X 90 sequence

herbal: Archaeologia pp. 364-393 / offprint pp. 16-45 / PDF pp. 22-51

object.description: Every surface exposed by Yale for Takamiya MS 46

relations.subjectLabel: Yale Digital Collections complete facsimile of Takamiya MS 46

assertion.notes: DIMEV's one broad witness range is retained, while direct image inspection separately maps the herbal and receipts around the shared f. 14v rubric boundary.

subject.description: DIMEV record 2343 and its historical TokyoTak46 witness record

reviewNotes: The cataloging boundary is Garrett's complete article-level edited composite, not one homogeneous medieval treatise and not the entire Anglia volume.
The two medieval works remain separate even though Garrett numbers them continuously and DIMEV 2343 collapses the complete Takamiya paired sequence under one witness record.

Garrett's direct Add MS 17866 transcription is the primary path; X 90 enters through Holthausen's comparison edition, while Takamiya MS 46 is a later-identified collateral witness and visual boundary control.
Whole-source caches retain the complete journals, complete offprint, complete DIMEV release, and all 114 Yale canvases; target parts are mapped separately and shared folio surfaces are explicit.
Witness dating, dialect, holding, and image terms remain attached to their physical or provider layers and are not generalized to the abstract works.

sourceLinks.format: Full-color IIIF page images, auditable ZIP bundle, and generated PDF

localCopies.label: Complete 114-canvas Yale facsimile of Takamiya MS 46
notes: Complete for all provider-exposed surfaces: covers, pastedowns, front and rear endleaves, ff. 1r-43v, and the laid-in parchment fragment recto and verso. Yale exposes no separately labeled spine or edge views.
The exact IIIF Presentation 3 manifest, 114 full-size images, and per-image URLs, sizes, and SHA-256 values are retained in the bundle inventory.
Representative images are full-color sRGB JPEGs around 2,200 x 3,760 pixels and visibly preserve parchment tone, red rubrics, initials, corrections, and binding features.

workPortion.label: The two CME00088 works within complete Takamiya MS 46
notes: The two works share f. 14v / canvas 34. The herbal closes with 'This medicine is proved well a plight'; the red rubric 'For the hede warke' and incipit 'If a man or woman more or lesse' begin the receipt collection on the same surface.
The opening red rubric 'Hic incipiunt virtutes herbarum' and first herbal lines appear on f. 3r / canvas 11; the receipt collection ends on f. 20v / canvas 46.

rights.basis: Yale's public-domain policy says Yale claims no rights in public-domain works and Beinecke welcomes public-domain use, while the exact IIIF item gives a general user-liability warning rather than an item license.
rights.component: Yale manuscript photographs, IIIF derivatives, generated PDF, and item presentation
notes: Yale requires or requests an appropriate Beinecke citation and places responsibility for legal assessment on the user.
The manifest's exact Rights field is a caution, not a Creative Commons license. Preserve citation and link-back data with any reuse.
The local bundle remains Git-ignored; local caching records research access and does not itself authorize repository redistribution.

lineage.summary: CME00088 is the checked-in copy of the University of Michigan encoding of Robert Max Garrett's complete 1911 article 'Middle English Rimed Medical Treatise,' printed on pages 163-193 of Anglia 34. Garrett's article joins a modern English preface to two distinct anonymous Middle English verse works copied continuously in British Library Add MS 17866, ff. 5r-21v: DIMEV 4171, a herbal on ff. 5r-16r, and DIMEV 2343, a collection of medical receipts on ff. 16r-21v. Garrett numbered the two as one, but DIMEV and the rubricated shared boundary in the complete Yale Takamiya MS 46 facsimile support retaining separate intellectual parts. Garrett directly transcribed Add MS 17866 and compared it with Ferdinand Holthausen's 1896 edition of the reversed pair in Stockholm KB X 90; Holthausen in turn newly collated X 90 against George Stephens's 1844 edition. Complete scans of Garrett, Holthausen, and Stephens, the complete DIMEV v1.0 source release, and the complete 114-canvas Yale Takamiya MS 46 facsimile are cached locally. No public facsimile was found for Add MS 17866 or KB X 90; current repository catalog, onsite, and whole-object reproduction routes are recorded instead.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous authors of two verse works
catalogSummary.date: Medieval composition dates unknown; surviving paired witnesses are dated from the later fourteenth century to ca. 1425, with conflicting individual witness dates; Garrett completed the article in 1909 and published it in 1911
catalogSummary.region: England; Add MS 17866 is scribally localized to Lincolnshire, while Takamiya MS 46 is described as East Anglian and by eLALME as southern Lincolnshire; composition place is unknown
catalogSummary.title: Middle English Rimed Medical Treatise

catalogingSubject.label: Middle English Rimed Medical Treatise
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Robert Max Garrett's complete article-level composite represented by CME00088: a seven-paragraph modern English preface, two distinct anonymous Middle English verse works transcribed continuously from British Library Add MS 17866, and Garrett's editorial dateline and signature. Garrett numbered the two poems as one because they are adjacent in the manuscript, but DIMEV records 4171 and 2343 and the rubricated shared boundary visible in Yale Takamiya MS 46 support separate work parts. This record is not one homogeneous medieval treatise, the complete Anglia volume, any one physical manuscript, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Garrett's comparative preface
contentParts.summary: Seven English prose paragraphs identify Add MS 17866, compare its two works and their order with Holthausen's X 90 edition, discuss the manuscript date and dialect, and list omissions and line correspondences.

contentParts.label: Of Herbs Twenty-Three
notes: The work ends and the receipt collection begins on the same Add MS 17866 f. 16r and Takamiya f. 14v surface.
contentParts.summary: A 753-line anonymous Middle English herbal in rhyming couplets, opening with leek and then treating betony, centaury, marigold, celandine, pimpernel, motherwort, vervain, dragonwort, aristolochia, agrimony, mint, sage, rue, wormwood, fumitory, eyebright, and other plants through medicinal, protective, ritual, and astrological uses.

contentParts.label: Collection of Medical Receipts in Couplets
notes: The first red rubric and incipit share the same physical surface as the end of the herbal in both directly mapped paired witnesses.
contentParts.summary: A 370-line anonymous Middle English collection of rhymed remedies divided by 29 rubricated headings for headache, eye and ear disorders, loss of speech, wounds, gout, swellings, dropsy, bleeding, fistulas, cancer, pox, bone pain, felon, and related complaints.

contentParts.label: Editorial dateline and signature
contentParts.summary: Garrett's University of Washington, Seattle dateline of 4 December 1909 and signature close the article.

assertion.notes: The interval preserves conflicting witness dates and does not identify one confirmed earliest witness or provide an exact composition date.

dateStatements.display: The paired works are attested by ca. 1400-1425 in Takamiya MS 46; an older KB catalog dates X 90 to 1350-1399, while Holthausen allowed a fifteenth-century date
Garrett dated the completed article Seattle, 4 December 1909
Garrett's Add MS 17866 article and edition were published in 1911
notes: This is the first verified publication of Garrett's article-level composite, not the first publication of the X 90 versions, which Stephens printed in 1844.

extent.description: Four cataloged content parts: a seven-paragraph prose preface, a 753-line herbal in one LG, a 370-line receipt collection in 29 rubricated LG elements, and one prose editorial closer. The complete XML has 31 PB and 256 MILESTONE elements. Counts describe this encoded representation, not a normalized critical edition of either work.

formStatements.description: Substantive modern English prose framing plus two Middle English verse works
Short rhyming couplets; the receipt collection is divided by rubric headings into 29 encoded line groups

genres.label: Medical compilation
notes: A modern article-level composite of two independent medical verse works, not one organically unified medieval compilation.

scope.description: Rubrics, medical maxims, prayers, and formulae embedded in the Middle English works
A short French rue charm or formula followed by an English explanation

notes: Garrett's singular article title and continuous lineation are edition-level choices; DIMEV's two work records and the shared rubricated manuscript boundary control the intellectual part model.
The prose preface and editorial closer are retained because CME00088 encodes the complete article, but their English language and prose form are not attributed to either medieval author.
Add MS 17866's Lincolnshire scribal localization and Takamiya MS 46's East Anglian or southern-Lincolnshire descriptions remain witness-level claims; no place of composition is inferred.

The complete Takamiya manuscript is locally cached and mapped at exact folio and canvas ranges. X 90 and Add MS 17866 remain request routes rather than falsely labeled undigitized or unavailable objects.

next_steps: Review current critical scholarship on DIMEV 4171 and 2343 and the Macer tradition.
Separate authorial dialect evidence from Add MS 17866, X 90, and Takamiya scribal language.
Do not assign one witness's production date or locality to the article-level composite or both abstract works.
openQuestions.question: When and where were the two verse works composed, and what source or compilation stages produced their divergent witness versions?

next_steps: Ask the British Library to reconcile its fourteenth-century date with Garrett and the MED.
Request complete color photography and a modern foliation and collation statement, identifying ff. 5r-21v separately.
Retain the exact supplied-image license before any Git or publication use.
openQuestions.question: How should Add MS 17866 be dated and described in current specialist cataloging, and can its complete physical object be photographed?

next_steps: Ask DIMEV whether this is a deliberate witness-unit convention or a record-link omission.
Retain the direct f. 14v rubric evidence and do not collapse the two intellectual works while the census structure is reviewed.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV record 2343 treat the whole Takamiya ff. 3r-20v paired sequence as one witness while record 4171 does not separately list its visibly bounded herbal?

next_steps: Keep all image caches Git-ignored and link the public Yale object while seeking written clarification for Git and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand reuse.
Record KB's pending response and any British Library delivery terms at the exact access layer.
Keep underlying medieval text status separate from modern image and provider terms.
openQuestions.question: What item-specific terms govern redistribution and print-on-demand use of Yale's public Takamiya images and any future British Library or KB facsimiles?

notes: A broad cultural and linguistic origin only; no exact place of composition is asserted.
DIMEV cites LALME/eLALME profile 1.100. This is not automatically the authorial dialect or composition place.

scope.description: Scribal language of the Add MS 17866 versions

notes: Yale says the opening poems show traces of East Anglian dialect; DIMEV/eLALME instead describes the historical Takamiya witness language as southern Lincolnshire, leaving the precise relationship open.

scope.description: Dialect traces in the Takamiya MS 46 versions

notes: Seattle is the place of Garrett's editorial completion, not a medieval place of composition.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous author or compiler of Of Herbs Twenty-Three
notes: The work draws on the Macer herbal tradition, but no named English author is supported.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous author or compiler of the verse medical receipts

scope.description: Article-level selection and continuous numbering of the two manuscript works
Direct transcription of Add MS 17866, ff. 5r-21v

notes: Garrett's article supports comparison through Holthausen's edition, not direct physical consultation of X 90.

scope.description: Comparison against Holthausen's printed X 90 edition

subjects.label: Medical astrology and prognostication
notes: Includes instructions keyed to lunar and planetary conditions and tests of life, death, or hidden knowledge alongside herbal uses.

subjects.label: Christian healing charms and ritual
notes: Includes prayers and gathering rites in the herbal and a Saint Nicasius pox charm in the receipt collection.

summaries.value: Garrett's 1911 article presents two adjacent but independent anonymous Middle English medical poems from British Library Add MS 17866. A prose preface compares their order and readings with the Stockholm X 90 versions. The first poem is a 753-line herbal combining medicinal plant properties with prayers, charms, and astrological gathering instructions. The second is a 370-line series of rubricated remedies for ailments from headache and eye disease to wounds, dropsy, cancer, pox, and bone pain. Garrett numbers them continuously as one, but the manifest preserves the two-work boundary and includes his concluding 1909 dateline and signature as editorial framing.

CME00088 represents Garrett's complete printed pp. 163-193 article, including all modern framing and both medieval works. The XML has one preface DIV2 and one poem DIV2; the intellectual work boundary occurs inside the poem division between its first LG and the first LG TYPE='part', after line 753. Add MS 17866 f. 16r and Takamiya MS 46 f. 14v are shared boundary surfaces.

notes: Garrett's article title is retained for the article-level composite; it is not presented as the original title of either medieval work.

titles.value: Middle English rimed medical treatise / [ed. Robert Max Garrett].

titles.supplied_by: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

notes: DIMEV records this as a title in the wider tradition; it is not claimed as the visible Add MS 17866 rubric.

titles.value: Mother of grass, whoso them seeks

work.summary: Garrett's 1911 article presents two adjacent but independent anonymous Middle English medical poems from British Library Add MS 17866. A prose preface compares their order and readings with the Stockholm X 90 versions. The first poem is a 753-line herbal combining medicinal plant properties with prayers, charms, and astrological gathering instructions. The second is a 370-line series of rubricated remedies for ailments from headache and eye disease to wounds, dropsy, cancer, pox, and bone pain. Garrett numbers them continuously as one, but the manifest preserves the two-work boundary and includes his concluding 1909 dateline and signature as editorial framing.
